name: Pediatrics: CH 23 Peds neuro and sensory DOs

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
Use the same approach as with adult patients
Exclude family members from care decisions
parents to provide experiences that the child can be successful in
scenarios.rationale: parents to provide experiences that the child can be successful in. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 30)

Use the same approach as with adult patients
performs aggressive assessment in looking for them
scenarios.rationale: performs aggressive assessment in looking for them. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 34)

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: Focus only on the child's medical diagnosis
Treat all children the same as adults
Include family in care decisions and use age-appropriate communication
scenarios.rationale: Family-centered care is essential. Include caregivers, use age-appropriate language, consider developmental needs. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Pain assessment in non-verbal children uses:
options: FLACC scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability)
Asking the child to describe pain
scenarios.rationale: FLACC is validated for infants/young children. FACES for 3+ years. Numeric for 8+ years. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The most reliable indicator of pain in infants is:
scenarios.rationale: Infants: observe behavioral cues (facial expression, body posture, cry quality). FLACC scale. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Which vital sign changes first in a deteriorating pediatric patient?
scenarios.rationale: Respiratory rate changes first in children (compensatory mechanism). BP is a late sign of decompensation. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the correct pediatric CPR ratio?
options: 30:2 for single rescuer, 15:2 for two rescuers
scenarios.rationale: Infant/child CPR: 30:2 single, 15:2 two rescuers. Compression depth: 1/3 chest depth. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The leading cause of death in children over 1 year is:
scenarios.rationale: Unintentional injuries (drowning, MVAs, burns) are #1 cause of death in children >1 year. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: When should car seats face rearward?
options: Until age 2 or until child exceeds weight/height limits
scenarios.rationale: AAP: rear-facing until age 2 or until exceeding car seat limits. Then forward-facing with harness. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the recommended daily water intake for a 5-year-old?
scenarios.rationale: Children 4-8 years: ~5 cups/day fluids. More with activity/heat. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Which is the best way to prevent childhood obesity?
options: Healthy diet and regular physical activity
scenarios.rationale: Prevention: balanced diet, 60 min/day physical activity, limit screen time, family meals. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child who is choking and cannot cough or speak needs:
scenarios.rationale: Choking: abdominal thrusts for conscious child >1 year. For infant: 5 back blows + 5 chest thrusts. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The safest car seat position for infants is:
scenarios.rationale: Infants: rear-facing until age 2 or until exceeding car seat limits. Back seat only. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: When should children transition to a booster seat?
options: When they outgrow forward-facing harness (typically 4-8 years)
scenarios.rationale: Booster: when child exceeds forward-facing harness limits (~40-65 lbs). Until seat belt fits properly (usually 8-12 years). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Drowning prevention includes:
options: Fencing pools, close supervision, CPR training
scenarios.rationale: Drowning prevention: 4-sided fencing, constant supervision, CPR training, water safety education. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Lead poisoning prevention includes:
options: Hand washing, removing paint chips, iron-rich diet
scenarios.rationale: Lead: hand washing, wet mopping, removing peeling paint, iron/calcium-rich diet. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Poison prevention: keep medications:
options: Locked up and in original containers
scenarios.rationale: Poison prevention: locked cabinets, original containers, childproof caps, poison control number posted. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The leading cause of death in children 1-14 years is:
scenarios.rationale: Unintentional injuries (MVAs, drowning, burns, poisoning) are #1 cause of death in children. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Bike helmet use should begin:
options: When child starts riding a bike
scenarios.rationale: Bike helmet: required for all ages. Proper fit: level on head, snug straps. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Match each developmental theorist with their key concept.
scenarios.rationale: Erikson: psychosocial crises. Piaget: cognitive development. Kohlberg: moral reasoning. (Slide 15-20)

scenarios.question: Complete the statement about pediatric care.
scenarios.cloze_text: Pediatric nursing requires [[BLANK1]] care and consideration of [[BLANK2]] in all interactions.
scenarios.rationale: Family-centered, age-appropriate care is fundamental in pediatrics. (Slide 10)

scenarios.question: Click on the normal developmental findings.
scenarios.highlight_text: The child demonstrates age-appropriate milestones. Growth follows expected percentiles. The child engages with caregivers appropriately.
scenarios.rationale: All are normal pediatric findings. (Slide 25)

scenarios.question: A child presents with concerning developmental findings.
scenarios.rationale: Early identification and family support are key. (Slide 30)

scenarios.question: A 4-year-old has a blood lead level of 14 mcg/dL. What is the priority nursing action?
options: No action needed, this is normal
Report to the provider and initiate lead exposure assessment
scenarios.answer: Report to the provider and initiate lead exposure assessment
scenarios.rationale: Lead level ≥5 mcg/dL requires intervention. Assess home for lead sources (paint, pipes), teach handwashing, iron-rich diet. Chelation is for higher levels (≥45 mcg/dL). (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: A child on high-dose prednisone has sodium of 150 mEq/L. What is the likely cause?
options: Medication side effect causing sodium retention
scenarios.answer: Medication side effect causing sodium retention
scenarios.rationale: Corticosteroids like prednisone cause sodium retention and potassium loss. Monitor for edema, hypertension. Teach low-sodium diet if needed. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: A child with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) has an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of 400/mm³. What precaution is indicated?
options: Neutropenic precautions (avoid crowds, hand hygiene, no fresh flowers)
scenarios.answer: Neutropenic precautions (avoid crowds, hand hygiene, no fresh flowers)
scenarios.rationale: ANC <500 = severe neutropenia. Risk for opportunistic infection. Avoid crowds, sick contacts, raw foods, fresh flowers/plants, and use strict hand hygiene. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: How should ferrous sulfate be administered to maximize absorption?
options: With milk to prevent stomach upset
With orange juice on an empty stomach
scenarios.answer: With orange juice on an empty stomach
scenarios.rationale: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) in orange juice enhances iron absorption. Give on empty stomach if tolerated (or with small snack). Avoid calcium/milk which inhibits absorption. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: Before giving digoxin to an infant, the nurse should check:
scenarios.rationale: Hold digoxin if HR <60-100 bpm (age-dependent). Check apical pulse 1 full minute. Notify provider if below threshold. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: A child taking oral prednisone long-term should be monitored for:
options: Hyperglycemia, weight gain, and infection risk
scenarios.answer: Hyperglycemia, weight gain, and infection risk
scenarios.rationale: Corticosteroids cause sodium retention (edema), hyperglycemia, weight gain, moon face, increased infection risk, and can suppress growth. Monitor glucose and weight. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: A child with acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis should follow which diet?
scenarios.rationale: Glomerulonephritis causes fluid retention, hypertension, edema. Low-sodium diet and fluid restriction help reduce edema and control BP. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: A child with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) should use nighttime splints for:
options: Knees, wrists, and hands to maintain function and reduce pain
scenarios.answer: Knees, wrists, and hands to maintain function and reduce pain
scenarios.rationale: Night splinting of affected joints (knees, wrists, hands) reduces pain and maintains range of motion. Encourage ROM exercises during day. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: A 2-year-old presents with barky cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarseness. What is the first-line treatment?
scenarios.rationale: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis): viral inflammation of larynx/trachea. Cool mist humidifies airways, corticosteroids reduce edema. Racemic epinephrine for severe stridor. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: An infant with bronchiolitis (RSV) is admitted. What is the priority nursing intervention?
options: Provide humidified oxygen and nasal suctioning
scenarios.answer: Provide humidified oxygen and nasal suctioning
scenarios.rationale: RSV bronchiolitis: supportive care. Infants are obligate nose breathers — suctioning clears secretions. Oxygen for hypoxemia. Hydration. Contact precautions. Antibiotics not indicated (viral). (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: Which pain scale is most appropriate for a 6-month-old nonverbal infant?
scenarios.rationale: FLACC is validated for infants/young children who cannot self-report. Scores 0-2 in 5 categories. FACES for 3+ years, numeric for 8+ years. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: An adolescent who is a Jehovah's Witness refuses a blood transfusion. What is the priority nursing action?
options: Respect the refusal and notify the provider and parents
scenarios.answer: Respect the refusal and notify the provider and parents
scenarios.rationale: Respect religious/cultural beliefs. Involve parents in discussion. Document refusal. Explore alternatives (erythropoietin, volume expanders). Ethics consultation if needed. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)

scenarios.question: An adolescent completed all immunizations through age 6. Which vaccine is needed now?
scenarios.rationale: Tdap is recommended at age 11-12 (or catch-up). Also: MCV4, HPV series. Adolescents who completed DTaP through age 6 need Tdap booster. (Quizlet: ATI CMS Peds Practice A)
